MAE enables the whole distillation framework to be efficient and effective
Our method is built upon MAE, a powerful autoencoder-based MIM approach. Specifically, the MAE encoder first projects unmasked patches to a latent space
MAE is based on the MIM approach, utilizing the MAE encoder and decoder to predict pixel values of masked patches.

The core element of MAE includes masking, where the image is divided into non-overlapping patches and a subset is kept for the MAE encoder while the rest is set as the predicting target for the MAE decoder.
The MAE encoder is a key component of MAE that operates on unmasked patches and reduces the computation cost of encoders.
The MAE decoder, in addition to receiving features from the MAE encoder, also receives mask tokens as input for reconstruction of image pixel values.
MAE directly reconstructs image pixel values using a simple mean squared error for calculating loss, differing from BEiT or MaskFeat.
Knowledge distillation is demonstrated to have extraordinary capabilities in learning high-capacity models efficiently and effectively in MAE.

In this work, knowledge distillation is combined with the MAE framework to efficiently acquire small and fast models with similar performance as powerful yet cumbersome models, specifically by directly applying knowledge distillation at the pre-training stage.
The whole pre-training and fine-tuning process in this paper exactly follow the default setup in MAE.
DMAE enables a much more efficient pre-training setup compared to MAE, with 100 (vs. 1600) training epochs and 95% (vs. 75%) masking ratio.
